data.title: Traditional Chinese parenting 101: sending a child on a solo mission
data.description:
“Be brave,” I told my 12-year-old daughter at the station steps. The words were as much for me as for her.
Train pass – check. Phone, wallet, debit card – check. Rendezvous after her piano lesson – check. “And don’t miss your stop!” It was our third run-through of the checklist.
“Dad, you’re such a fusspot. I’ll be fine,” she said, cringing as she hoisted her schoolbag. We’d made that trip to her piano teacher’s studio, near Town Hall station in the Sydney central business district, many times before. She swiped her pass and strode through the turnstiles. For a moment, as she began her maiden solo journey – just before she vanished into the crowd – she seemed taller, shoulders back, as if the station platform had given her a little lift. And I recognised in her air the same exhilaration I’d once felt somewhere in the streets of Kowloon 35 years earlier.
I was eight when I visited Hong Kong with my family in 1990. My grandfather, a businessman then in the furniture trade, had gathered us for Christmas at the Mariners’ Club in Tsim Sha Tsui, a tall blue building that once stood on the southern tip of the Kowloon peninsula. On our second morning, he summoned me to his room. I remember the purposeful glint in his eye – the look of a veteran tutor issuing an important assignment.
“When I was your age, I fetched my grandpa’s newspaper every morning. On my own.” He tapped a finger on the coffee table. “I’ll show you the route today, walk it with you tomorrow; after that, you’ll do it yourself.”
It was his version of “watch once, try once with supervision, then go solo” – a teaching method common to his generation, forged by experience, hard knocks and the belief that children should be toughened up for the world. Years later, I would encounter this tough-love approach again in medical school.

We set out before breakfast. Our hotel sat beside Signal Hill Garden, its stairs rising to a hill that overlooked the neighbourhood. We’d visited the lookout the day we arrived. The newsagent was inside a mall across a T-intersection, about 200 metres away. Because I wasn’t allowed to jaywalk, we went to the next crossing and doubled back. The route seemed simple enough. The following morning, with grandpa by my side, I navigated it correctly.
On the third morning, I went alone. I found the newsagent without trouble, but on the way back I took a wrong turn. The streets turned unfamiliar. I had no phone, no map and I couldn’t speak Cantonese. I gripped the newspaper tighter. Beyond a canyon of towers, I caught the outline of Signal Hill. Remembering how it overlooked the district, I entered the park and climbed to the top. From there, I saw the hotel’s familiar blue and found my way back.
A little late, breathless but triumphant, I stepped into the lobby clutching the newspaper. My grandfather was waiting. When I told him I’d lost my way and used Signal Hill to get my bearings, his concerned expression softened into a small, satisfied smile. My mother, who hadn’t been privy to the mission, was not at all impressed.

That contrast was formative; it still guides my parenting. My grandfather, very much in the traditional Chinese mould, believed independence came from shouldering responsibility early; to him, controlled risk was training for adulthood. My mother, a new migrant from Shanghai raising her only child in late-20th-century Australia, absorbed the safety-first ethos of an era defined by “stranger danger”, checklists and caution. Back home, I was mostly wrapped in cotton wool; that day in Kowloon was my coming of age.
Modern Chinese parenting balances two instincts. Confucian values prize duty to family, respect for elders and teachers, and the cultivation of character. Parents today, especially in the diaspora, wrestle with equally important Western ideals of choice, individuality and freedom.
Growing up in Australia, I lived in the in-between: neither fully bound by one tradition nor free of the other. Grandpa never said so, but I sensed he thought small struggles – like getting a little lost – were the best way to build character. Mum, by contrast, insisted that I check in with her whenever I went out. This tension endures in how I parent. I want my children to stand tall, yet know they are not alone; to take risks, but with safety nets.

Encouraging responsibility is vital for growth. My daughter’s solo train ride wasn’t in Kowloon, but letting go was harder than I’d imagined. Like my mother, I reached for checklists; like my grandfather, I knew it was time to let her find her own way. After her first solo mission, my daughter’s face shone with the same pride I’d felt on Signal Hill. Soon her younger brother will want his turn. I won’t send him into Kowloon. I’ll look for his version instead: small, safe adventures that help him find his bearings, like going to school camp or mapping the route on our next family holiday.
Parents forever walk the line between safety and freedom. Every generation shifts that line. For my wife, our children and me, one truth endures: independence grows from trust. The art of parenting – Chinese or Western – is to teach, to trust and to let go.

data.title: Safety fears as Japan prepares to restart nuclear plant ‘built on tofu’
data.description:
Campaigners against nuclear energy have condemned Japan’s decision to resume operations at the Kashiwazaki-Kariwa power plant, claiming that the facility will be unable to withstand a major earthquake as it was “built on tofu”.
Tokyo Electric Power Co (Tepco), the operator of the Niigata prefecture plant, on Wednesday applied for a final examination of the facility to the Nuclear Regulation Authority (NRA). Approval is likely to be a formality as the prefectural assembly already gave the nod on Tuesday.
Tepco, which has been fiercely criticised for its handling of the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant disaster in March 2011, intends to restart one reactor at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa on January 20.
It would be the first of Tepco’s nuclear reactors to resume since three of Fukushima’s six reactors melted down after a magnitude 9 offshore earthquake unleashed devastating tsunamis.
Tepco has been working hard in recent years to convince the local government and residents of Niigata prefecture that it has made upgrades to secure the site from natural disasters.
Its president, Tomoaki Kobayakawa, told the media that as the operator “responsible” for the Fukushima accident, the company was now “committed to prioritising safety” at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa.

Many people remain unconvinced.
“The plant was famous for having been built on tofu – the bedrock is 30 metres (100 feet) below the surface – with many fault lines running through the site as well as offshore,” said Shaun Burnie, senior nuclear energy specialist for Greenpeace.
“Kashiwazaki-Kariwa is one of the world’s largest nuclear plants and the only one in theory capable of operating for Tepco. So restarting operations has been a priority for both the company and the government for more than a decade,” Burnie told This Week in Asia.
“But resistance from Niigata residents, the scale of safety issues and the incompetence of Tepco have undermined all efforts until now.”
History repeating itself?
Nearly 15 years after the Fukushima plant was the site of the second-worst nuclear accident in global history, Burnie warns that serious problems are being overlooked again.
Even during the preliminary planning stages, in the mid-1970s, it was apparent that ground conditions at the site by the Sea of Japan were not suitable, he said.
That was shown in July 2007, when the Chuetsu-oki earthquake struck. The magnitude 6.6 quake occurred on a previously unknown offshore fault line about 11km (7 miles) off the coast, followed 13 hours later by a 6.8 temblor around 330km (205 miles) to the west.
The nuclear plant, 19km (12 miles) due south from the epicentre of the initial quake, registered a peak ground acceleration – essentially how much the ground shook – of 6.8 metres (22 feet) per second squared in reactor No 1. The design specification for achieving a safe shutdown is 4.5m/s², while the restart level for key equipment in the plant is 2.73m/s².


Burnie says the original design specifications at the site “turned out to be gross underestimates”, with the quakes causing damage of varying degrees at 3,000 places within the plant.
A fire in an electric transformer caused the most serious damage. It left a pall of black smoke over the plant and alarmed local residents. There were also a number of leaks of mildly radioactive water and spills of radioactive cobalt-60, iodine and chromium-51 from overturned storage drums.
Given the events of 2007 and 2011, Burnie says local residents’ concerns are justified, particularly as new issues have come to light.
“We should have no confidence in Tepco assurances on safety at Kashiwazaki-Kariwa,” he said. “For example, two months after the NRA had approved the reactors’ basic safety assessment in late 2017, Tepco revealed that … the site under the emergency hydrogen ventilation buildings at units five, six and seven was vulnerable to liquefaction.”
Tepco “immediately came under pressure to explain the scale of liquefaction at the site, prove that it does not extend to the reactor buildings and how it was that the NRA was not informed prior to granting approval”, Burnie said.
Equally, he said, the NRA had to explain how it failed to identify liquefaction as a problem for a site before it granted basic safety approval.
A series of security incidents have also been cause for concern. There have been accusations of poor handling of sensitive documents, malfunctioning intruder detection systems and an employee borrowing a colleague’s pass to enter the restricted main control room in 2020.
Shaken by fears
But the greatest risk remains the region’s susceptibility to seismic activity.
“There are multiple seismic fault lines in the area of the Kashiwazaki-Kariwa site, including large-scale submarine active faults,” Burnie said. “The enormous seismic risks at the site remain unresolved and are certain to dominate the debate about the safety of any reactor restart, including in ongoing legal challenges.”
The decision has the stamp of approval of the national government, with Niigata Governor Hideyo Hanazumi reporting developments to Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi in Tokyo on Tuesday. Takaichi said she “deeply respects his decision” and emphasised that the government would ensure that Tepco was vigilant about safety.

In a statement issued on Tuesday, Tepco said it would “carefully cooperate with all inspections performed by the NRA while also continuing to prioritise safety and steadily making each and every preparation for a restart”.
Takeshi Sakagami, a member of the Citizens’ Association for Monitoring Nuclear Regulation, said that a recent survey conducted by Niigata prefecture determined that 60 per cent of local residents believed that “conditions are not right” to resume operations at the plant.
“The governor explained that support should increase as understanding deepens,” Sakagami said. But “opponents counter that the will of the majority of residents today is opposition to a restart and that opposition is firm”.
Sakagami believes the government is hastening to restart the reactors “because they fear that Japan’s nuclear technology will fall behind the rest of the world”.
For him, the direction that Japan should take is simple.
“I believe Japan should abandon nuclear power and boldly shift towards renewable energy sources and energy conservation,” he said.

data.title: Trump says Ukraine’s Zelensky ‘doesn’t have anything until I approve it’ ahead of talks
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said he expects to meet US President Donald Trump in Florida on Sunday in pursuit of a deal to end Russia’s nearly four-year invasion, though the latest comments from Moscow raise doubts about how close a final agreement really is.
Zelensky told reporters on Friday that he would discuss sensitive issues with Trump including the future of the Donbas region in Ukraine’s east and the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant.
The Ukrainian leader said he plans to refine Kyiv’s agreements with the US to the maximum extent possible, potentially as soon as Sunday’s discussions, while the comprehensive 20-point peace plan would then require input from Russia and Europe.
Trump confirmed that Zelensky – and, separately, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu – is coming to the US to meet him during an interview with Politico published on Friday. The US president also asserted that he has veto power over any potential Ukraine-Russia peace deal.
“He doesn’t have anything until I approve it,” Trump said, referring to Zelensky. “So we’ll see what he’s got.”
The Ukrainian leader has said that his country’s framework deal with the US was “almost ready”, and signing it would depend on the meeting with Trump.
Trump also said that he plans to speak with Russia’s President Vladimir Putin “soon” and “as much as” he wants. The president anticipated that conversations with both leaders would “go good”.


Zelensky said he would like European leaders to be present online during his meetings with the US. He held a flurry of calls on Friday with major allies, including German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, Nato chief Mark Rutte and Finnish President Alexander Stubb, among others. A conference call between Trump, Zelensky and European leaders could take place on Saturday, Axios reported, without saying where it received the information.
“Everyone in Europe must be on the same page in defending our European way of life, the independence of our states and peace in Europe,” Zelensky said in a social media post after speaking with Merz.
The larger 20-point peace plan, which Zelensky characterised as “90 per cent ready”, cannot be signed without Russia and Europe, who are parties to the possible deal draft alongside Ukraine and the US, he said.
A total of five documents developed by negotiators from Ukraine and the US are being tweaked on a daily basis, Zelensky said. Those cover topics such as a post-war security plan for Ukraine and an economic recovery deal likely to spawn several separate agreements.
Some issues remain, though, that can only be discussed at the leaders’ level, he said.
Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Ryabkov described Kyiv’s latest plans as “radically different” from the main points Moscow has discussed with the US in recent weeks, according to a report in TASS.
He went on to say that Ukraine and its European allies have “redoubled their efforts to torpedo” any actual accord to end a conflict that began with Russia’s invasion in February 2022, but he also hinted that Christmas Day talks had yielded some progress.
“December 25 marked a point at which the sides moved closer to a settlement and a breakthrough is now needed,” Ryabkov said, according to TASS.
Earlier this week, a person close to the Kremlin said that Moscow will seek major changes to the US-Ukraine plan, including more restrictions on Kyiv’s military.

Separately, Ukraine is convening a working group on organising post-war elections, Zelensky said. A potential referendum on aspects of the nation’s future is one of the elements of the 20-point plan.
Zelensky on Thursday spoke with US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner for almost an hour, in what the Ukrainian president called “a very good conversation”.
The White House did not provide a readout of those talks and has not commented on a potential meeting with Zelensky. Trump is spending the Christmas break at his Mar-a-Lago club in Florida.
Zelensky said on Friday he also wants to discuss with Trump how the US could apply more pressure to Russia if Putin does not agree to sign the peace deal.
Putin continues to press Russia’s maximalist demands, including for Kyiv to give up land in Donbas that his troops have failed to capture for more than a decade of hostilities.
Ukraine rejects that demand, fearing that surrendering the heavily fortified area would leave it vulnerable to a new Russian attack.
However, Zelensky signalled earlier this week that he was open to the idea of creating a special economic zone in the eastern part of Donbas, as envisaged by the US, and pull his troops – but only if Russia does the same.
As pressure to reach an end to hostilities increases, Kremlin forces continue regular missile and drone strikes on Ukraine, with a recent focus on Odesa, while Kyiv’s forces have targeted Russia’s oil refineries and gas plants.
At least one person was killed and several civilians injured in the Black Sea city on Friday, with port infrastructure, warehouses and two civilian vessels damaged, Deputy Prime Minister Oleksiy Kuleba said on Telegram. Air barrages were also aimed at Kharkiv in the northeast, Cherkasy in central Ukraine and Mykolaiv in the south, among other locations.

data.title: China teacher suspended for cursing at noisy pupils, hoping they would catch the flu
data.description:
A primary teacher in China has been suspended from her job for cursing at “naughty” pupils and saying that she hoped they would all get the flu.
The outburst appeared in a chat group and has gone viral online.
The teacher, surnamed Li, is a music tutor at Gaoxin District Experimental School in Tongchuan, Shaanxi province, northwestern China, Jiupai News reported.
Her comments in a matchmaking chat group with 100 members caused outrage.

“The students are so naughty and so noisy. I feel sorry for my eardrums. I hope they all get the flu. Get out and go back home immediately,” Li said in the chat group.
In reply to some people who were unhappy with her comment, Li said: “I do not care as long as I receive my income. It is fine with me if you tip off my bosses. But if you had to endure this job, you would not do that,” Li added.
When others advised her not to hope that her pupils catch the flu, Li insisted the jibe was just part of the point she was making and should not be focused on.
“After all, these pupils are not your kids. It is none of your business,” Li said.
In response to criticism that she would not last long in the job with such an attitude, Li said: “I am afraid I must remind you that I have a permanent employment contract.”

It is not known how long Li has been teaching.
In China’s government departments and public-funded enterprises and institutions, a small proportion of staff are hired in a permanent capacity.
This effectively means they have a job for life unless they make a serious mistake.
Some members of the chat group released the conversation online, triggering a public backlash against Li.
Li has been temporarily suspended from her job, according to her school.
A spokesman from the local education authority told the media on December 15 that they have launched an investigation into the incident.
“After we complete the investigation, we will release the results to the public,” the official said.

Since November, the flu has been rampant in primary and secondary schools across the country.
Some classes have been suspended because so many pupils have fallen sick. Some teachers ask children to wear masks at school.
The story sparked a wave of condemnation among Chinese netizens.
“Just because you are permanently hired, you can be so unethical. Get out of the teachers’ group. You do not deserve it,” said one person.
“The government should investigate carefully how she got this job,” said another.

data.title: Genomic sequencing puts Hong Kong at forefront of cancer care innovation
data.description:
Genomic sequencing has helped Hong Kong identify the right treatments for cancer patients more quickly, according to an expert who also suggested that the city could be a “super-linker” between mainland China and the rest of the world in drug development.
Aya El Helali, a clinician-scientist in medical oncology at the University of Hong Kong, praised how collaboration within the Greater Bay Area had opened up opportunities to run clinical trials faster and at lower costs – factors that appealed to global pharmaceutical companies.
The use of genomic medicine, which examines a person’s genetic information to tailor healthcare, could help to not only diagnose rare diseases but also determine the effectiveness of medications.
“We’re allowing all patients to have access to potential therapies that might affect their survival,” said El Helali, who spoke in a sideline interview at the International Symposium on Genomic Medicine in Hong Kong last month.
El Helali, a clinical assistant professor with HKU’s department of clinical oncology, said the use of genomic sequencing had significantly shortened the time needed to identify suitable treatments for brain cancer patients to just one or two years – compared with the traditional approach that took five to 10 years.
Sharing her research on patients with glioblastoma, the most aggressive type of brain cancer, which was “very difficult to treat”, the doctor said in-depth genomic sequencing was performed on patients’ tumours to find the specific “keyhole” that a new drug could target.
The drug was then administered to patients, followed by another round of genomic sequencing on the tumour.
“What this allows us to do is to understand … is this drug getting into the brain tumour for these patients?” she said.
“If it is not, then we stop exploring this drug completely because we know it’s never going to work for our patients.”
As this sequencing can be conducted during the first phase of a clinical trial, which usually takes about one to two years, researchers will be able to determine much earlier whether the drug may work on a larger scale.
“This saves us from investing another five to eight years in futile larger trials,” El Helali said.
She added that the traditionally adopted research approach required testing through phases of one to three clinical trials, with researchers learning only at the end – after five to 10 years – whether the drug was effective.
“For brain tumours, we sometimes discover too late that the drug couldn’t effectively cross the blood-brain barrier to reach the tumour, meaning all that time was invested for a result that didn’t help patients,” she said.
While global data show that 90 per cent of glioblastoma patients could die within 15 months of diagnosis, the HKU professor said some patients who underwent genomic sequencing had survived for five to seven years.
While work is still under way to determine the effectiveness of the newer approach for glioblastoma patients, El Helali said other cancers – including those of the ovaries, breasts and lungs – could also benefit from genomic sequencing.
Patients recruited for a clinical trial do not need to pay for the sequencing. However, those unable to join a matching study may have to pay between US$200 and US$4,000 (HK$1,550 to HK$31,000), depending on the scale of the test.
As an adviser to the Greater Bay Area International Clinical Trial Institute (GBAICTI) – a Hong Kong government initiative launched in 2024 – El Helali said the city had several advantages that could make it a bridge between the mainland and the global community.
“We do leverage a lot of international standards that we do have in Hong Kong that make it very, very unique,” she said.
She added that clinical trial studies published by Hong Kong researchers were highly respected worldwide because of the quality of data collection and strong intellectual property protection.
“The super linker is going to be in the GBAICTI, with Hong Kong being the bridge to allowing a lot of the Chinese biopharmaceutical companies to enter into the rest of the world, and for international drugs to be able to enter into China,” she said.
With closer collaboration between Hong Kong and other mainland cities in the bay area – which has a population of more than 86 million – El Helali said it could become easier to recruit patients for clinical trials at lower costs, making the region more attractive to pharmaceutical companies.
As the Hong Kong Genome Institute is conducting a large-scale genome sequencing project that has already recruited more than 52,000 participants, the professor said pharmaceutical companies could be interested in the data to assess drug development needs for the Asian population, which accounts for 60 per cent of the global total.
“The superconnector for Hong Kong for the rest of the world is the fact that we have not [only] opened up our clinical trial potential, but we have also opened up our genomic sequencing potential as well,” she said.

data.title: China warns foreign forces deploy AI deepfakes to stir panic and steal data
data.description:
Foreign forces have used deepfakes and other artificial intelligence (AI) technologies to create public panic and obtain sensitive information, posing a threat to China’s national security, the country’s top intelligence agency has warned.
Deepfake technology, which uses AI’s deep learning algorithms to simulate and forge images, audio and video, can be abused or used maliciously to endanger individual legal rights, social stability and national security, the Ministry of State Security (MSS) said in an article on Friday.
It said some “hostile foreign anti-China forces” had used this technology to generate fake videos and attempted to spread them in China to “mislead public opinion and create panic”.
The ministry added that using AI could lead to data leaks and security risks. It cited a case in which staff had used open-source AI tools that permitted users to view files without a password, allowing someone with a foreign IP address to access and download sensitive data.
The ministry also warned of algorithmic bias, where large language models might amplify discrimination if the training data reflects social biases or under-represents certain issues.


To combat these risks, the ministry said users should be cautious when granting access permission, avoid using web-based AI to process sensitive data, refrain from saving payment passwords and disable data sharing and cloud storage. They should also monitor their “digital footprints”, periodically clear AI chat histories, update antivirus software and be alert when asked to provide personal identification and bank account details.
“Security is the prerequisite for development, and development can safeguard security,” the ministry said.
“Only by understanding technology and using it safely can we ensure that large-scale AI models can become a positive force in driving social progress.”
It cautioned users to improve their security awareness, exercise caution when granting permission to large model software, and report suspicious activity, such as AI models that steal personal information or send sensitive data abroad.
The ministry has frequently warned of security risks in AI-related fields. In March, it said companies and government bureaus must prevent data leakage when training AI models and be alert to criminals who use loopholes to destroy smart systems in factories, financial institutions or energy plants, which can result in economic losses and accidents.
In October, China approved an amendment to its Cybersecurity Law that added a section on AI security and development. It requires improved ethical guidelines, strengthened security risk monitoring and assessment, and tighter security regulations related to AI.

data.title: Greater ambition: how Hong Kong and bay area neighbours could host Asian Games, Olympics
data.description:
Wheels were turning in more ways than one at a packed Hong Kong Velodrome last month when cyclist Ceci Lee Sze-wing rode to her third gold medal of the 15th National Games.
Roared on by home spectators, it symbolised the successful realisation of the decision for the city to co-host the Games alongside Guangdong and Macau, its partners under the Greater Bay Area development plan.
It created a scene officials hope to see replicated on an even bigger scale: historic achievements in front of passionate sold-out crowds at world-class venues, projecting the bay area’s capabilities and advantages to the world.
The multi-sport Games required cross-border cooperation on infrastructure, logistics, security, manpower and commerce, and may prove to be a precursor. A working group of business leaders and sports officials has been building support behind the scenes for the region to host an Olympics, Asian Games or other major sporting event.

Bach to the future
Thomas Bach, the honorary life president of the International Olympic Committee (IOC), visited Hong Kong during the National Games and when asked about a potential bid, told the region to “go for it”, adding he believed it had “all the ingredients” to stage a global version and it “could be a very important candidate” for future Olympics.
“If you look at the scope of the National Games, they are bigger than the Olympic Games,” Bach said.
“You have many world-class sports facilities, you have a sports-loving public, you have all the ingredients it needs, so one day I think it would be very good to be a candidate for the Olympic Games.”
Bach had nothing to lose in making those remarks, and said something similar in South Africa during a visit in the closing days of his presidency in October last year.
“Africa is on the rise and South Africa has the stability, the infrastructure and vision to stage an Olympics,” he told a media briefing in Johannesburg.
“I’m here to highlight the ever-growing importance of a young continent like Africa in the Olympic movement and to support this development.”
Less than a month after Bach made his comments at Kai Tak Stadium, South Africa announced its intention to bid for the 2036 Games, adding itself to a list of as many as 10 potential candidates, including India, Turkey, Qatar and Chile.
However, Kirsty Coventry, Bach’s successor as IOC president, had paused bidding in June to allow for a review of the selection process.
Still, Kenneth Fok Kai-kong, vice-president of the Sports Federation and Olympic Committee of Hong Kong, China (SF&OC), called Bach’s comments “important encouragements”.
The National Games, Fok said, “laid an important foundation for future bids to host international events”.
“Bidding for the Olympics is an exciting vision, but it also involves certain complexities,” he added. “We hope to learn from the 15th National Games … and accumulate richer experience for the Greater Bay Area’s Olympic bid.”
Accumulating “richer experience”, as Fok put it, would seemingly need the region to host other multi-sport, multi-day events first. But opportunities for doing so appear few and far between.
The next available Asian Games are in 2038 and 2042, with Japan hosting in 2026, followed by Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
Los Angeles in 2028 and Brisbane four years later leave 2036 and 2040 as the open slots for the Olympics, with the one in 11 years’ time the focus of a potential Hong Kong bid from the 2036 Working Group.

Cost of doing business
A coalition of officials from across business, sport and government, the group has released a detailed document arguing the region could host the Olympics with a “cost-neutral” model.
Among its arguments was the infrastructure already in place, which would eliminate the most common source of cost overruns, while ambitiously predicting a multibillion-dollar revenue stream that could offset operational expenses and protect taxpayers.
As a starting point, the group cited the substantial contribution from the IOC for Brisbane 2032, which is estimated at around US$1.8 billion, more than nine times the Hong Kong government’s HK$1.48 billion budget for the 2025-26 financial year.
Paris, though, provides a dose of reality, with the final public cost of its 2024 Summer Games and Paralympics hitting €6.6 billion (US$7.7 billion), of which €3.63 billion went to infrastructure projects. Separate from that overall total was the €4.4 billion in private financing that funded the local organising committee.
According to the group, offsetting any outlay would be the broadcasting rights, which are forecast to be some US$900 million (HK$7.04 billion) in 2032. The expectation, the group argues, is that China’s massive domestic market would see that figure dwarfed by comparison.
Other revenue streams would be ticket sales, which in Paris brought in €1.333 billion, and sponsorship from major global brands, which has already reached US$1.6 billion for LA 2028, with organisers targeting US$2.5 billion.
However, economist Simon Lee Siu-po sounded a note of caution, suggesting the timing of such an undertaking was far from ideal, given Hong Kong’s economic situation.
Lee pointed to an overwhelming number of international studies that concluded the economic benefits of hosting the Games were often “questionable”, causing a “winner’s curse”, under which a host city ultimately overpays and sees an abnormal or negative return on investment.
“The Hang Seng Index, property index, retail sales, catering sales, tourist numbers, tourist spending … Hong Kong has yet to return to the pre-Covid level,” he said, adding there were also fiscal constraints.
“Hong Kong is facing a serious deficit now, and needs to raise debt. Now is not the appropriate time to apply for the Olympic Games.”
Momentum is building, however. Timothy Fok Tsun-ting, the SF&OC’s president, has predicted Hong Kong hosting the general assembly of the Association of National Olympic Committees next December will “provide an opportunity to show what is happening in Hong Kong sport”.
“They hear about it, but it’s very different to see it. There are many ways to expose Hong Kong as a wonderful place for sports exchanges … and it could be the beginning of us getting more international events.”

If you build it
As the working group has pointed out, much of the infrastructure needed to host a major Games already exists across the region, not just when it comes to venues, such as Kai Tak Stadium, but transport between all the major hubs too.
And there was a more optimistic perspective on the “winner’s curse” from Terence Chong Tai-leung, executive director of the Lau Chor Tak Institute of Global Economics and Finance at the Chinese University of Hong Kong.
He said it primarily affected places “without well-built infrastructure”, forcing them to spend heavily on new stadiums and transport networks. Hong Kong as a highly developed city largely bypassed this requirement.
“We already have stadiums,” he said. “We already have a very well developed infrastructure including high-speed trains, railways and so on … the investment will not be that high.”
Chong believed the most substantial return would be intangible: revitalisation of the city’s global reputation.
“Everybody would be watching Hong Kong, so they will think Hong Kong is still a city that can attract tourists,” he said. “I think the major benefit is to build up our international image again.”
Timothy Fok has said previously that a successful cross-border National Games could inch the region closer to the “ultimate prize” of welcoming an Olympics, something “everybody is chasing”.
But he acknowledged that Hong Kong needed more facilities, including for top-class swimming and tennis, although he played down the amount of building required.
“Governing bodies don’t want you to build expensive facilities for one-off Games,” he told the Post in September. “The most important thing is creating a legacy – not having two weeks of competition, then no plan.”
He also said: “The government is seeing the success of our athletes and becoming more generous, flexible and cooperative. We’re having more major events and putting Hong Kong on the map, and we can do more.”

Cross-border challenges
That is not to say there aren’t challenges associated with the Greater Bay Area hosting an Olympics or Asian Games, even if China has already done so multiple times.
The major difference is that while Beijing’s 2008 and 2022 Olympics and the Asian Games in Hangzhou in 2023 were all held wholly in mainland China, a bay area version would require the movement of athletes, officials, goods and potentially hundreds of thousands of spectators between Hong Kong, Macau, Shenzhen and elsewhere.
While freedom of movement has eased over the years, it is still far from smooth, or certain, depending on when citizens of some countries arrive at the crossing points.
But Patrick Lau Wing-chung, professor in the department of exercise and public health at Baptist University, said the region’s advantages outweighed challenges around cross-border transport and differing legal and regulatory regimes.
“According to the report from the National Games, 90 per cent of existing venues can be directly used for international competitions,” he said. “This will greatly reduce the cost of rebuilding.”
He suggested the biggest challenge would be posed by the influx of people, saying that over the Olympics’ 16 days, Paris received 7 million visitors, with up to 730,000 people watching events on the same day.
“There were 207 countries and regions. That is much more complex than the National Games,” Lau said. “The cross-border procedures are not simple.”
Those visitors may need to cross the border to watch different events.
“The number may reach up to 400,000 people crossing in one day. Can Hong Kong handle it?” he asked. “At most, Hong Kong can handle about 300,000 people a day now, and that’s mostly mainland Chinese. If large numbers of foreigners come, the pressure will be very, very high.”
Party of four
Chinese officials seemed to lay the groundwork for an Olympic bid on November 24, when the government announced that Hong Kong, Guangdong, Macau and the General Administration of Sport of China had agreed a partnership aimed at strengthening sports cooperation and promoting integrated development.
Known as the “Four-Party Agreement”, it pledged to consolidate the success of the National Games, and its provisions included further joint hosting of sports events.
The agreement made official work that had already been taking place before and during the Games, which featured a cross-border marathon and the implementation of a “seamless customs clearance”, which officials said created a “zero delay, zero contact, zero wait” zone.
Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu believed it signified “a new stage of cooperation among the three places”.
“Under the strategic framework of the Four-Party Agreement, the HKSAR Government will capitalise on the distinctive advantages of ‘one country, two systems’ to promote the integrated development of sports in the Greater Bay Area,” he added.
“We will deepen the cooperation in areas such as consolidation of sports resources, joint hosting of sports events, integration of sports industries and nurturing of talents, thereby contributing to the building of China as a leading sporting nation.”

data.title: Chinese historical myths that are accepted as fact, and why they have endured
data.description:
Last week, I noted that the contents of the Albatross file cast doubts on the popular narrative that Singapore was abruptly “kicked out” of Malaysia on August 9, 1965.
The documents show that separation was not a unilateral expulsion but the outcome of months of secret, bilateral planning by senior leaders on both sides. The idea originated within Singapore’s own leadership in 1964, when figures such as then Minister of Finance Goh Keng Swee (who opened the file) judged that it was politically untenable for Singapore to remain in Malaysia.
Far from being hasty, separation was carefully negotiated with the federal government in Kuala Lumpur, albeit under conditions of extreme secrecy, with only a handful of people kept in the loop.
Many commentators have been quick to surmise that the account of Singapore’s sudden expulsion was a myth concocted and perpetuated by the ruling establishment, chiefly to emphasise the city state’s vulnerability and, by extension, the need for a strong hand at the helm. As yet, there is no firm evidence that this was the case.

Historical myths do not usually arise from a single source or motive; they emerge through different mechanisms and for different reasons.
China offers no shortage of such examples. A relatively recent myth holds that Sun Yat-sen “led” the 1911 Revolution that overthrew the Qing dynasty. In reality, Sun was not even in China when the Wuchang Uprising, the trigger of the revolution, broke out in October 1911. He was in the United States raising funds and seeking international support, and learned of the uprising only after it had already begun.
The revolution itself was the product of long-term structural crises within the Qing state, combined with the actions of military officers, local revolutionary cells, constitutionalists and provincial elites.
Sun’s Tongmenghui, or Chinese Revolutionary Alliance, provided ideological inspiration and organisational networks, but it neither planned nor directed the decisive events on the ground. Sun’s elevation as the Provisional President of the Republic of China in early 1912 was largely symbolic, reflecting his international reputation and revolutionary credentials.
This complex, multi-actor process was later simplified, resulting in the exaggeration of Sun’s role.

Another enduring myth of modern Chinese history claims that the Qing dynasty lost the First Sino-Japanese War of 1894-95 because Empress Dowager Cixi (1835-1908) diverted funds from the Imperial Chinese Navy’s budget to rebuild the Summer Palace.
The story originated in the polemics of exiled reformers after 1898, especially Liang Qichao, and later hardened into “common knowledge”, complete with claims that tens of millions of taels were squandered on the palace.
Evidence-based scholarship tells a different story. While historians broadly agree that funds administered in the navy’s name were used for the Summer Palace, careful research shows that the scale was far smaller and the circumstances far more complex than the narrative suggests.
Most estimates place total spending at less than 10 million taels, with only part of that sum plausibly described as “diverted”. Much of the money raised under the navy’s banner was never intended for the navy in the first place.
China’s defeat stemmed less from Cixi’s extravagance than from structural fiscal weakness and poor governance.

One cannot discuss historical myths in China without mentioning the Three Kingdoms period (220-280). Because the 14th-century novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms, together with the folk stories that circulated long before its publication, was so widely read and retold, fictionalised versions of people and events from that era are still accepted as historical fact today.
Historical myths arise and endure for different reasons. Singapore’s “kicked out” narrative may owe less to deliberate fabrication than to simple neglect. In the early, forward-looking decades after independence, few paused to distinguish between historical fact and myth. Besides, key records remained classified.
Historical myths are not always lies. More often, they are simplifications that persist because they are useful, easy to remember and emotionally satisfying.

data.title: ‘Lost my future’: Myanmar’s ‘charade’ election dims hopes of diaspora
data.description:
Taya says her life was looking up as Myanmar crept out from decades of isolation.
She had a job in marketing, enough money to shop in Yangon’s new malls and fuel her quiet dreams of opening a small bakery, as her generation enjoyed unprecedented economic and personal freedoms.
But that was before Myanmar’s short-lived experiment with greater democracy ended in a coup in 2021.
With the military once more in charge, the economy is in free fall, and inflation has surged to well over 20 per cent. The country is mired in an ongoing civil war, which has driven millions to flee from their homes – many of whom are overseas – and forced thousands to pick up arms.
Taya, 24, is among them. She moved to Bangkok in February, fearing a conscription order into the Tatmadaw – as the military is known. The junta announced last year that men aged 18 to 35 and single women aged 18 to 27 would have to serve military service in accordance with a 2010 military conscription law.
“I have lost my future,” she said, as her shift at a downtown bar in the Thai capital ended. Her small bartending wages allow her to avoid fighting for a military that has been killing the pro-democracy rebels she supported.
“I won’t go back. I can’t go back. It’s a trap,” she added. She has requested to be referred to by one name to avoid the junta identifying her.

On Sunday, the junta will begin a phased election, seeking to turn back the clock to before it allowed some reforms, including a straitjacketed form of democracy.
In the two most recent elections held in 2015 and 2020, the military was humiliated at the ballot box as Aung San Suu Kyi’s National League for Democracy achieved back-to-back landslide victories.
The last triggered the coup by senior general Min Aung Hlaing.
This election will start in Yangon and continue in dozens more townships in January. As a supposed exercise in democracy, observers say the election is a farce, as it would rubber-stamp the military’s continuous dominance over a quasi-civilian government.
Just six military-approved or aligned parties will contest the polls nationally, with no protests, no real opposition and no election campaigning allowed.
This week, United Nations rights chief Volker Turk warned the military to stop using “brutal force to compel people to vote” and end the arrests of protesters “expressing any dissenting views” after scores of activists were rounded up ahead of the election.
The statement by the high representative on behalf of the European Union was equally damning, saying that “these elections are only pursuing bolstering legitimacy objectives for the junta”.

The limited scope of the election – covering townships in Yangon, the capital Naypyidaw and other central parts of the country the junta controls – means most of the population are unable to cast a vote.
For Min Aung Hlaing, the coming election is crucial to securing his and the Tatmadaw’s future following the farcical 2020 polls, in which the military was guaranteed to wield power under the 2008 constitution that it scripted, according to analysts.
“Aung San Suu Kyi and the NLD were never meant to win, let alone govern,” Richard Horsey, International Crisis Group’s senior adviser on Myanmar, told This Week in Asia. “The coup and this election are about erasing that outcome and rerunning 2020 with a result the military can live with.”
Min Aung Hlaing has vowed to steward a handover of power to the new civilian government.
But few expect anything other than a fig leaf of change, with generals still calling the shots, including for new cabinet appointments, while allowing China, Russia, India and Myanmar’s other foreign partners to claim they are dealing with a legitimate government.
“The generals are trying to restore what they see as the ‘proper’ functioning of the 2008 constitution – a system they designed to guarantee military dominance behind a civilian facade,” Horsey said. But he warned the election “will not provide an off-ramp from chaos”.

Nation destroyed
The civil war has killed tens of thousands since the 2021 coup, with virtually all of the country consumed by conflict at some point.
Over 3.6 million people had been displaced from Myanmar, the UN said this month, with the number expected to skyrocket to 4 million next year. Over 16 million people – among them 5 million children – would need “life-saving assistance and protection in 2026”, the UN added.
The weakened military continues to rely on air strikes and artillery to intimidate breakaway areas. The conflict and recent natural disasters have pushed one of Asia’s poorest countries further to the edge.
With the economy hit by Western sanctions, the junta has turned to plundering Myanmar’s rare earths and other natural resources, while taking a cut from unfettered cyber scams and drug production.
This year, the junta has clawed back some lost territory as ethnic armed groups retreat along the country’s eastern border, seemingly to appease China, on whose supply chains and finances they depend.
But the election is unlikely to solve the violence. “We are not participating in this sham election. It will prolong the military dictatorship, and we will never submit to it,” Padoh Saw Taw Nee, a spokesman for the Karen National Union (KNU), told This Week in Asia.

The KNU, which has been fighting Myanmar’s various governments for nearly 76 years, said the military was laying waste to civilian areas. It hosts 1.2 million displaced people in its territory near the Thai border, but can cover only around 20 per cent of their needs as the scale of the humanitarian crisis overwhelms its resources.
“There is no freedom of speech, freedom of assembly and this is not representative of the will of the people. After the sham election, the situation will become worse and worse,” the spokesman added.
With millions of young people – the economic engine of the country – forced overseas to work or dodge the draft, experts warn that Myanmar’s sclerotic decline is set to deteriorate further.
“The election will change nothing,” said Htwe Htwe Thein, an associate professor with a special focus on Myanmar at Curtin University in Australia.
“The economy will remain in collapse, the civil war will continue, and people will keep suffering. Western sanctions will stay in place because this charade fools no one.”
Rumours of a possible military mutiny against Min Aung Hlaing have quickly fizzled out despite massive losses of men and weapons in the military.
This is because the Tatmadaw has been pushing its cultlike doctrine of how the country can only move forward if the military is strong, according to Horsey.
“This belief, rarely questioned within the officer corps, speaks to how Min Aung Hlaing and the current crop of leaders see Myanmar, even as the country unravels around them.”

data.title: Need for speed: Chinese researchers unveil new technique for near-instant AI video creation
data.description:
A new technique called TurboDiffusion claims to be able to accelerate artificial intelligence video-generation speeds by up to 200 times at scale, without compromising overall image quality, according to a recently published paper.
The technique reduced the time needed to generate a five-second, standard-definition video clip from more than three minutes to just 1.9 seconds – a nearly 100 times acceleration – when it was tested on a consumer-grade system with Nvidia’s RTX 5090 graphics processing unit, according to researchers from Beijing’s Tsinghua University, AI model developer Shengshu Technology and the University of California, Berkeley, in a paper published late last week.
The time it took to generate a high-definition, five-second video on the same machine was cut from nearly 80 minutes to 24 seconds, or about 200 times faster, according to one experiment cited in the paper.
Currently, it takes between three and five minutes for a user to generate a standard five-second video on Shengshu’s Vidu platform. OpenAI had also said its text-to-video model Sora takes several minutes to render a short clip.
TurboDiffusion underscores the rapid strides made by Chinese researchers in advancing AI video-generation technology since OpenAI introduced Sora in February 2024.
Accelerated video creation represents a significant shift, as the use of video-generation models would no longer be a bottleneck in the production process, according to industry analyst Kyon Xu.


The researchers attributed the accelerated pace of video generation to model training innovation, including the use of sparse linear attention – in which an AI model focused on the most important parts of data, rather than process everything at once. That would cut processing time and computing costs significantly.
“TurboDiffusion signals a shift from ‘can generate’ to ‘can generate instantly’, unlocking real-time possibilities for AI video creation across industries,” the AI Native Foundation – a group that fosters the inclusive and equitable integration of AI across diverse sectors – said in a post on X on Friday.
According to the foundation, TurboDiffusion would help empower enterprises to do “commercial-scale video creation with reduced cost and faster iteration”.
For researchers, the post suggested that the technique “offers an open-source, cutting edge benchmark for fast, high-fidelity video synthesis”. It was also ideal for developers “for interactive and real-time applications”.
TurboDiffusion is fully open-sourced via developer platform GitHub.
